reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's suffrage would constitute a 'serious menace' and that the public should be made aware of this danger before any action is taken.

quotes.text: the serious menace which would be the result if women had a vote
it was a subject of sufficient importance to warrant its being discussed in the country at a general election before any step was taken

reasons.rationale: The speaker objects to the procedural manner of introducing such a major constitutional change, arguing it is wrong to propose it through a private member's bill on a Friday afternoon.

quotes.text: utterly wrong and indefensible to propose a change of this magnitude in the Bill of a private member, introduced oh a Friday afternoon

reasons.rationale: The speaker deflects accusations of illiberalism by claiming opponents are motivated by a genuine desire to do what is best for women, framing opposition as a moral and honest conviction.

quotes.text: they were actuated by a desire to do what was best for women
those who were opposed to this Bill were opposed to it on any ground which was ungenerous or illiberal

top_quote.text: the serious menace which would be the result if women had a vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker implies female voters lack the independence and capacity for sound political participation compared to working men.

quotes.text: female voters who might not possess that feeling of independence which was generally attributed to working men

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that broad enfranchisement, including of women, is premature until education is more widely spread.

quotes.text: Great numbers of persons would be disfranchised by the Bill until education was more thoroughly spread than it was at present

top_quote.text: female voters who might not possess that feeling of independence which was generally attributed to working men

reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses proportional representation, university representation, and minority rights in the context of electoral reform, not women's political rights specifically.

quotes.text: It is an essential part of democracy that minorities should be adequately represented.
I welcome that part of the Bill which tends to reduce the unjust privileges of wealth.

top_quote.text: It is an essential part of democracy that minorities should be adequately represented.

reasons.rationale: The speaker acknowledges that women's interests need representation and has already made a concession to add an extra woman member to the committee.

quotes.text: I have very readily given the concession of adding one woman
I hope there will be more than one woman among those who are nominated as members

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues against mandating specific numbers of women on the committee, suggesting women are already represented through their elected (male) union representatives and that demanding more female members reflects an 'obsession about women' rather than genuine need.

quotes.text: have this obsession about women
Large areas of women's labour are represented by men; the women themselves have elected them

reasons.rationale: The speaker dismisses fears that women will be 'ruthlessly outvoted' by men on the committee, implying that warnings about male bias are overstated and destabilizing to the committee's intended balance.

quotes.text: one woman, or even two women, will be ruthlessly outvoted by these brutal men
The position has been to some extent overstrained by those who

top_quote.text: Large areas of women's labour are represented by men; the women themselves have elected them

reasons.rationale: The Prime Minister signals willingness to accommodate the Women's Suffrage Bill, describing himself as receptive to pressure for its consideration.

quotes.text: With regard to the Women's Suffrage Bill , the right hon. Gentleman, so far as I am concerned, is pushing an open door.

top_quote.text: the Women's Suffrage Bill , the right hon. Gentleman, so far as I am concerned, is pushing an open door

reasons.rationale: The speaker defends women's representation by highlighting that women constitute over half the population, implying they deserve proportional political representation.

quotes.text: women are more than 50 per cent. of the population
embarrassed by the lack of representation of women in their ranks

reasons.rationale: The speaker condemns a heckled remark about 'hermaphrodites' as impolite and unacceptable, defending women's dignity in political discourse.

quotes.text: They might bear that in mind when they make some of their less polite remarks

top_quote.text: embarrassed by the lack of representation of women in their ranks

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speaker is arguing for extending compulsory military service (conscription) to Ireland, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: compulsory military service in Ireland
why do we not go over to Ireland and bring in all the men who are knocking about at home doing nothing

top_quote.text: compulsory military service in Ireland

reasons.rationale: The speech is about opposing a divorce reform Bill, focusing on whether guilty parties should be able to force innocent spouses into divorce, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the guilty party should be able to force, providing financial arrangements are made, the innocent party
marriage is defined not only in the churches but also according to civil marriage

top_quote.text: the guilty party should be able to force, providing financial arrangements are made, the innocent party

reasons.bucket_open: sex discrimination in trade union/employment law
reasons.rationale: The speaker references sex discrimination and equality of sexes, but in the context of trade union membership rules and employment legislation, not women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: I can speak with some authority on this matter of sex discrimination
if she believed in the equality of the sexes and proper opportunities

top_quote.text: if she believed in the equality of the sexes and proper opportunities

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights the practical success of positive mechanisms used by a political party to achieve high female representation in the Welsh Assembly and Scottish Parliament.

quotes.text: A positive mechanism was used by a political party to ensure that the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe
there are more women than men in the Cabinet of the Welsh Assembly

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the progress in women's representation in devolved institutions as a positive achievement worth celebrating and emulating.

quotes.text: great progress has been made in this country in setting up the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament

top_quote.text: A positive mechanism was used by a political party to ensure that the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about criticizing a British statesman's foreign policy record, particularly regarding Italy, Austria, and Piedmont, with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: this eminent person is now Her Majesty's Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs
the same apparent truckling to Austria, and the same morbid desire for active interference in Italy

top_quote.text: this eminent person is now Her Majesty's Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that dropping the unique focus on women in aid priorities is a mistake, emphasizing that women bear the heaviest burdens in the poorest societies.

quotes.text: It drops the unique focus on women, yet women and their children make up 80 per cent. of all refugees
in the poorest societies women and girls bear the heaviest burdens of illiteracy and hunger

reasons.rationale: The speaker references the Government's own stated aim to promote the status of women as a key purpose of aid, arguing that budget cuts undermine this goal.

quotes.text: to promote the status of women and to help to tackle environmental problems
The Government's aim to help the poor—especially women—has wide public support

top_quote.text: It drops the unique focus on women, yet women and their children make up 80 per cent. of all refugees

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights historical exclusion of women from voting and standing for Parliament as evidence that the unreformed Lords is outdated and undemocratic.

quotes.text: no women could vote, let alone stand for Parliament
our country has changed beyond recognition, our society has changed beyond recognition and it is long past time that our Parliament changed too

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the hereditary Lords as almost entirely male and unrepresentative, implying that reform would produce a more inclusive and gender-diverse legislature.

quotes.text: Almost all those people are men; almost all are white
750 people are Members of the House of Lords by accident of birth

top_quote.text: no women could vote, let alone stand for Parliament

reasons.bucket_open: Northern Ireland political process / party exclusion
reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about the Northern Ireland peace process, the DUP's exclusion from Hillsborough talks, and access to government documentation — not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the Women's Coalition and the Alliance party—have had the right of access

top_quote.text: the Women's Coalition and the Alliance party—have had the right of access

reasons.rationale: The speech focuses primarily on women's health services, NHS spending, cancer screening, and nursery provision in Scotland, not on women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the Government have increased spending on the National Health Service in Scotland by 20 per cent.
There is no sign of any discrimination against women entering medicine in Scotland.

top_quote.text: As the only Scottish woman in the House of Commons

reasons.rationale: The speaker defends suffragettes' moral character by highlighting their patriotic sacrifice in suspending their campaign during wartime to support the Government.

quotes.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support
militant and nonmilitant suffragettes attacked the Government with all their force

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the history of the suffrage movement to argue it should not be disparaged by unfair analogies, implicitly affirming the legitimacy of their cause.

quotes.text: Is he not rather hard on his colleagues in the militant suffrage movement

top_quote.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support

reasons.rationale: The speaker strongly advocates for equal pay for equal work and equality of opportunity for women in industry, criticizing opponents of these principles.

quotes.text: against the principle of equal pay for equal work and equality of opportunity for women in industry
totally opposed to any idea that women should have equal rights with their fellow workers

reasons.rationale: The speaker dismisses arguments that women have lesser abilities than men as ludicrous and outdated.

quotes.text: To suggest that women have not the same abilities as men is ludicrous
That is true of all workers, whether they are men or women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues the Bill will practically help low-paid women workers achieve at least equality with the lowest-paid male worker, calling current pay disparities a scandal.

quotes.text: It would be a great incentive to those women to know that they should not be receiving any rate lower than that of any low-paid male worker
It is an absolute scandal that any employer in the country today should pay such low wages

top_quote.text: To suggest that women have not the same abilities as men is ludicrous

reasons.rationale: The speaker affirms women's full right to religious office and denies Parliament's right to prevent it, framing this as a matter of equal rights.

quotes.text: I am fully committed to women's right to be priests and bishops in the Church of England.
I deny Parliament's right to prevent that from happening.

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the suffragette legacy and historical precedent of active struggle as the model for women to achieve their full rights.

quotes.text: Women who want that right must follow the course of the suffragettes and others who fought for their rights.
all gains by women and all gains of progress in religious freedom have been achieved when people did something

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the Bill as entrenching limits on women's advancement, arguing the current measure is only a 'tiny step forward' that blocks further progress.

quotes.text: it represents a tiny step forward, although it is also an entrenchment of a law designed to see that that step cannot lead further.

top_quote.text: I am fully committed to women's right to be priests and bishops in the Church of England.

reasons.rationale: The speaker defends the 'flapper vote' (women's enfranchisement) against Tory critics who had denounced it, expressing belief in democracy and women's right to vote.

quotes.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote
members of which a short time ago were denouncing their leader for according the "flapper" vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker calls for electoral reform including proportional representation, arguing the current system distorts representation, which implicitly affects women's fair political participation.

quotes.text: this Parliament is not a true representation, but an immense distortio
the result of the election clearly called for electoral reform

top_quote.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speaker (Balfour) is primarily concerned with the procedural confusion of having multiple electoral reform bills sent to different committees simultaneously, not with women's political rights.

quotes.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time
tinkering with this subject, and then on the top of it all, not bringing them before the same Grand Committee

top_quote.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time

reasons.rationale: The speaker mocks the use of tradition-based arguments against constitutional change by comparing the Prime Minister's rhetoric to that of die-hard Tories who opposed women's suffrage, implying such arguments are discredited.

quotes.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last
it would destroy in years what has been built up in centuries

top_quote.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last

reasons.rationale: The speaker is arguing about unemployment benefit eligibility for workers locked out during trade disputes, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: men or women, paying into an unemployment insurance scheme
not to give strike benefit to men or women on strike

top_quote.text: men or women, paying into an unemployment insurance scheme

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues against restricting women's access to abortion under the 1967 Act, framing it as protecting women's rights to safe and legal terminations, but this is about reproductive/healthcare rights, not political rights or representation.

quotes.text: restrict the right of women to an abortion under the Abortion Act 1967
people whom we are seeking to protect are those who need early, safe and legal abortions

top_quote.text: restrict the right of women to an abortion under the Abortion Act 1967
Parliament is asked to vote £173 million and 325,000 men and women to this force.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women should be represented on local authorities, noting broad cross-party agreement on this principle.

quotes.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities
a certain number of women should be on these local authorities

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights that local authorities are failing to co-opt women despite vacancies, and that upcoming education reforms make women's inclusion on these bodies especially urgent.

quotes.text: The local authorities are not using their power of co-option and putting women on their bodies
take an active part in local administration, especially in regard to education

top_quote.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that taking extreme measures against militant suffragists would backfire by encouraging the movement rather than suppressing it, implying the constitutional women's suffrage movement deserves space to grow.

quotes.text: the constitutional supporters of Women suffrage are greatly gaining ground
action which would have one inevitable result only, that of giving a vast encouragement to the movement, which he wishes to suppress

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes panic legislation against suffragists, warning that harsh government action would only inflame and strengthen the women's suffrage cause.

quotes.text: We always hear that appeal for panic legislation at a moment when certain people suppose their political opponents are not administering the law as they ought to
the violent section of the women are daily losing public support, and, what is more, are daily losing funds

top_quote.text: the constitutional supporters of Women suffrage are greatly gaining ground

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the immigration rules formally and deliberately discriminate against women by denying them the same right as men to bring foreign spouses into Britain.

quotes.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.
fundamental distinction between the treatment of women resident in this country and that of men who enjoy the same condition

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the qualified statement of rights is a political manoeuvre that will in practice keep genuine married couples separated, undermining any real benefit to women.

quotes.text: many partners in genuine marriages will be kept separate
the statement, which is qualified virtually out of existence

reasons.rationale: The speaker notes that the Conservative manifesto explicitly promised to end the Labour concession allowing husbands to join wives, revealing the partisan tradition underlying the discriminatory rules.

quotes.text: We shall end the concession introduced by the Labour Government in 1974 to husbands and male fiancés.
a direct denial of the policy on which the Conservatives had chosen to fight the election

top_quote.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about the speaker's 30-year parliamentary career, constituency history, and Labour party politics, with no discussion of women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: This month, I shall have been in the House 30 years
It covers 300 square miles, 38 parish councils and 40-odd towns and villages

top_quote.text: This month, I shall have been in the House 30 years

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's interests as voters must be considered equally and carefully in deciding election timing, and criticises the House for struggling to represent women's perspectives.

quotes.text: I believe that in the constituency of Richmond, Yorks there are people whose interests would be disadvantaged by an early election—the female electorate
It is difficult for this House to consider women's interests because the vast majority of hon. Members—through no fault of their own—are men

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that election timing and conditions affect women voters' practical ability to participate in the political process.

quotes.text: ensure that that election would take place at a time when its conduct and the weather conditions would be as conducive as possible to political discussions
They must try to overcome that disadvantage and look at matters through our eyes now and again

top_quote.text: It is difficult for this House to consider women's interests because the vast majority of hon. Members—through no fault of their own—are men

reasons.rationale: The speaker cites a prior ruling he made that introducing women into the Franchise and Registration Bill would completely alter its character, using this as a precedent for how far-reaching amendments change a bill's nature.

quotes.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill
the Franchise and Registration Bill and the introduction of women into it

top_quote.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that genuine equal opportunities for men and women must be ensured, opposing all-women shortlists as discriminatory while still supporting women's representation on merit.

quotes.text: the Government must learn…that there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women
all-women lists were found to be clearly in breach of the treaty of Rome and the Sex Discrimination Act 1975

reasons.rationale: The speaker credits the previous Conservative government's policies (Opportunity 2000, flexible labour markets) with increasing women's participation in public life, professions, and boardrooms.

quotes.text: Opportunity 2000, which has put more women into public life and indirectly resulted in greater numbers of women in the professions
More work opportunities for women arise today as a result of the previous Government's commitment to flexible labour markets

reasons.rationale: The speaker emphasises that women Conservative MPs were selected on merit, implying women are fully capable of serving in Parliament without needing gender quotas.

quotes.text: I and the other 13 women who represent the Conservative party in this House were selected on merit, and not simply to fulfil the gender quota

top_quote.text: there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women have rendered equally effective service to the state during the war and therefore have an unanswerable claim to enfranchisement if new franchise qualifications based on state service are introduced.

quotes.text: they presented to me not only a reasonable, but, I think, from their point of view, an unanswerable case
we cannot possibly deny their claim—that during this War the women of this country have rendered as effective service

reasons.rationale: The speaker acknowledges women's practical contributions—filling munition factories, replacing men who went to fight—as grounds supporting their claim to a voice in post-war industrial reconstruction.

quotes.text: they fill our munition factories, they are doing the work which the men who are fighting had to perform
have not the women a special claim to be heard on the many questions which will arise directly affecting their interests

reasons.rationale: Despite acknowledging women's claims, the speaker ultimately argues against opening franchise questions during wartime, warning it would divert national effort and destabilise the focus needed to prosecute the war.

quotes.text: nothing could be more injurious to the best interests of the country, nothing more damaging to the prosecution of the War
that at this stage of the War that that should be thrown on the floor of the House of Commons

top_quote.text: we cannot possibly deny their claim—that during this War the women of this country have rendered as effective service

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about supplementary benefits legislation, cohabitation rules, one-parent families, mobility allowance, and industrial injury benefits — none of which is framed in terms of women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: there are now 700,000 one-parent families in real need
the change in the cohabitation rule that will be effected by one of the later clauses of the Bill

top_quote.text: the change in the cohabitation rule that will be effected by one of the later clauses of the Bill
